Algeria.text: Algeria scraped through the group stage on the strength of a breathless 3-3 draw with Austria — Mahrez equalising twice, the second deep into stoppage time, before Kalajdzic's answer in the sixth minute of added time reduced them to third place and the anxious wait for a best-loser berth. That rollercoaster finish does not inspire confidence ahead of a Round of 32 date with Switzerland on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT; the Swiss are a composed, well-organised side and will arrive as measured favourites against an Algerian defence that conceded seven across the group. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana await in the Round of 16, with Argentina potentially looming in the quarterfinal — a path that demands far greater defensive discipline than Algeria have so far shown.

Argentina.text: Argentina swept through Group J without dropping a point, and the 3-1 defeat of Jordan was a characteristically composed performance — two first-half goals putting the match to bed before a Jordan consolation briefly complicated the scoreline, with Messi adding the gloss late to confirm the margin. Cape Verde await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a tie the world champions are expected to navigate without serious alarm, though the African side are no mere tourists. Survive that, and Australia or Egypt could follow in the Round of 16, before a quarterfinal draw that might serve up Colombia or Switzerland — and lurking further still, the semifinal bracket holds Brazil, England, or France among those who could finally furnish a genuine reckoning.

Australia.text: A goalless draw with Paraguay to close the group stage — functional, if short of conviction — was enough to send Australia through as runners-up in Group D. They meet Egypt in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a tie that looks like Australia's best opportunity: the Socceroos enter as modest favourites against a side rarely considered among the continent's elite, though African teams at a World Cup have a habit of defying tidy assumptions. Survive that, and the Round of 16 on Tue Jul 7 09:00 PT could bring Argentina — a vastly more forbidding proposition and a reminder of just how steep the mountain becomes from there.

Austria.text: Austria scraped through Group J in the most nerve-shredding fashion imaginable — Kalajdzic's equaliser deep in stoppage time salvaging a 3-3 draw with Algeria after Mahrez had twice turned the game on its head, the second time with three minutes left on the clock. They advance as group runners-up, but the performance offered little comfort ahead of what comes next. Spain await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a formidable proposition for any side and a near-mountain for an Austria team that has looked shakily porous at the back; the Spaniards arrive as heavy favourites and will expect to control the tie from first whistle. Survive that, and Croatia or Portugal lurk in the Round of 16 — either one a further stern examination of whether this squad has genuine knockout-stage substance.

Belgium.text: Belgium closed the group stage with something close to a statement: Trossard's brace had the game settled well before the hour, Bruyne and Lukaku added gloss in the final stretch, and New Zealand's late consolation did nothing to disturb the impression of a side moving through the gears comfortably. Senegal await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a genuine test, not a formality, given the Teranga Lions' athleticism and African football's recent upward curve, though Belgium's quality in the final third makes them clear favourites. Survive that, and Bosnia & Herzegovina or the USA could follow in the Round of 16 before the draw opens onto a potential quarterfinal collision with Portugal or Spain — the kind of fixture that would tell us whether this Belgian generation has finally shed its nearly-men reputation.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed the group stage with something close to authority, leading Qatar 2-0 inside 35 minutes and weathering a brief scare before Mahmic's 80th-minute third put the result beyond doubt — a convincing send-off that secured their passage as one of the best third-placed sides. They meet the host nation USA in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a steep assignment: the Americans carry home-crowd momentum and significantly greater tournament pedigree, making Bosnia decided underdogs in what figures to be a feisty, physical tie. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal could await in the Round of 16 — with Portugal or Spain lurking as potential quarterfinal opponents, names that would test any side left standing.

Brazil.text: Brazil squeezed past Japan in a Round of 32 tie that was far less comfortable than expected — Sano's 29th-minute opener put the Seleção on the back foot, Casemiro levelled after the break, and it took Martinelli's stoppage-time winner to spare their blushes. The Round of 16 brings either Ivory Coast or Norway on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT, a tie Brazil should negotiate as clear favourites despite the Japanese scare, though a Norway side built around physical menace and set-piece threat would demand a sharper defensive display. Survive that, and the quarterfinal path could run through England or Mexico — either of whom would constitute a genuine examination of whether this Brazil team has the composure to match its undeniable talent.

Canada.text: Canada survived rather than convinced against South Africa, Eustáquio's stoppage-time winner turning ninety-odd minutes of anxious stalemate into relief at the final whistle. The Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT brings either Morocco or the Netherlands — the Dutch, should they arrive, represent a significant step up in class and would enter as clear favourites, while Morocco offer a more navigable but hardly comfortable path for a Canadian side still finding its rhythm. Get through that and a quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT could pit them against France, which would be a stern examination of just how far this generation has genuinely come.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde navigated Group H without losing a match, though three successive draws — the last a goalless stalemate against Saudi Arabia — paint a portrait of a side that defends with discipline but has struggled to convert pressure into goals. That frugality will be tested to its absolute limit in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, when they face Argentina: by any measure the heaviest assignment in this bracket, a continental giant against whom Cape Verde will need to be at their most resolute and opportunistic. Survive that, and Australia or Egypt await in the Round of 16, before a quarterfinal draw that could produce Colombia or Switzerland as credible but navigable opposition.

Colombia.text: Colombia closed out Group K with a disciplined goalless draw against Portugal — a composed, professional performance that suggested neither side had much to prove, with the points already secured. They finished top of the group and carry genuine momentum into the knockout rounds. Ghana await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a winnable assignment for a Colombian side that has looked well-organized and potent in attack, though Ghana are never without menace at a tournament. Win that, and Algeria or Switzerland could follow in the Round of 16 — with Argentina potentially looming in the quarterfinal as the tie that would define Colombia's tournament.

Croatia.text: Croatia edged past Ghana in a nervy group-stage closer, Sucic's first-half strike giving them control before Ghana levelled late, only for Vlasic to restore the lead seven minutes from time — a win that required a second wind. That backdrop makes Thursday's Round of 32 date with Portugal on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT a genuine examination: Ronaldo's side carry the weight of expectation and Croatia will need to find a more composed performance to trouble them. Survive that, and Spain or Austria lurk in the Round of 16, with France or the Netherlands possible obstacles should Croatia reach the semifinal.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao closed their group stage campaign with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé striking early and then again just past the hour to make it a controlled, comfortable evening for the Elephants. The result confirmed what the tournament had largely borne out across three matches: Curaçao, plucky debutants on the greatest stage, were outgunned at this level. They exit with a single point, a goal difference of minus nine, and the distinction of having competed — if not quite threatened — against top-tier opposition in Group E.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 0-3 defeat to Mexico that was effectively settled inside the opening quarter-hour of the second half, Chávez and Quiñones striking within six minutes of each other before Fidalgo's stoppage-time finish applied a flattering coat of finality to what had already been a comfortable Mexican evening. The Czechs finished fourth in Group A with a single point and a goals difference of minus four, scraping through as one of the better third-placed sides before their tournament ended here, emphatically, on the wrong side of a mismatch they could never quite solve.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo came from behind to beat Uzbekistan 3-1 in their final group game, a result that looked precarious when Shomurodov struck early but ultimately turned into a convincing finish — Wissa's penalty and late second sandwiching a Mayele goal to make it comfortable by the end. That third-place finish nonetheless earns them a Round of 32 meeting with England on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a daunting assignment by any measure: the Leopards arrive as considerable underdogs against a side of far greater resources and tournament pedigree, and will need to replicate and surpass that second-half authority. Should they conjure an upset, Ecuador or Mexico await in the last sixteen, with Brazil or Ivory Coast potentially looming in the quarterfinals beyond that.

Ecuador.text: Ecuador closed out their group stage with a result that will reverberate well beyond their own camp — coming from behind to beat Germany 2-1, conceding inside two minutes to Sané before Angulo levelled early and Plata sealed it with a composed finish thirteen minutes from time. That comeback win, against one of the tournament's heavyweights, earned them a third-place berth and sets up a Round of 32 clash with neighbours Mexico on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT — a fixture loaded with continental rivalry, and one in which Ecuador arrive as the side carrying the greater momentum despite their underdog billing. Survive that, and England or DR Congo await in the Round of 16, with Brazil or Norway potentially lurking in the quarterfinal — the kind of bracket that rewards audacity, and Ecuador have already shown they possess it.

Egypt.text: Egypt's group stage ended with a scrappy, unsatisfying draw against Iran — Saber's fifth-minute opener was swiftly cancelled out by Rezaeian nine minutes later, and neither side could find a winner. They go through as Group G runners-up nonetheless. Australia await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a tie Egypt will be cautiously favored to win but which carries genuine risk against a Socceroos side capable of disrupting better-fancied opponents. Survive that, and Argentina could loom in the Round of 16 — a test of an entirely different order.

England.text: England closed out Group L with a controlled 2-0 victory over Panama, Bellingham and Kane striking within five minutes of each other midway through the second half to settle a game that was professional rather than electric. DR Congo await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT — a winnable assignment on paper, though England will need sharper early intent than they showed against Panama to avoid manufacturing tension. Should they progress, Ecuador or Mexico loom in the Round of 16, with a potential quarterfinal collision with Brazil adding genuine menace to the latter stages of a draw that could demand England's very best before the final on Sun Jul 19 12:00 PT.

France.text: Ousmane Dembélé settled the Norway contest inside half an hour, completing a first-half hat-trick before Doué added gloss deep in stoppage time — a 4-1 scoreline that flattered no one and announced France as the group stage's most ruthless finishers. Sweden await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, a fixture France enter as heavy favourites given the gulf in tournament pedigree, though Swedish sides have a stubborn habit of making their betters uncomfortable. Beyond that, Paraguay loom in the Round of 16, and a quarterfinal draw that could produce Morocco or the Netherlands suggests the real examination arrives in July.

Germany.text: Germany's tournament nearly unravelled against Paraguay in the Round of 32 — Enciso's first-half opener unsettled a side that had cruised through Group E, and though Havertz levelled after the break, the match never truly submitted to German control, ending 1-1 before Paraguay prevailed 4-3 on penalties. That shootout defeat means Germany are eliminated, their World Cup concluding in the round of 32 — a brutal, unceremonious exit for one of the competition's historically dominant nations, brought down not by a tactical masterclass but by a penalty lottery against unfancied opponents.

Ghana.text: Ghana's group stage ended with a chastening defeat to Croatia — a Sucic opener giving the Europeans control, and though Luckassen pulled one back midway through the second half, Vlasic's late strike seven minutes from time sealed the points and confirmed Ghana's exit from the group in third place. They advance to the Round of 32 all the same, where Colombia await on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT — a genuine test against a side carrying significantly more pedigree and continental weight, making this a tie in which Ghana must be considered underdogs. Survive that, and Algeria or Switzerland could follow in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal meeting with Argentina would represent the tournament's full weight bearing down.

Haiti.text: Haiti's final group game against Morocco ended 4-2, a scoreline that flatters neither side in quite the right way — the Haitians led briefly, equalized before half-time through Isidor, yet conceded four times across a match Morocco ultimately controlled with growing authority. Two late Moroccan goals in the final stages confirmed what the second half had suggested: Haiti were outclassed and outrun. They finish bottom of Group C without a point, their tournament defined by an 8-2 aggregate against them across three matches, and exit a World Cup that exposed the gulf between their ambition and the level required.

Iran.text: Iran's group stage ends with a 1-1 draw against Egypt — a match in which they surrendered the initiative inside five minutes before Rezaeian levelled early in the second quarter, and neither side could force a winner. Three draws across three games told the full story of a tournament in which Iran were never beaten but never convincing, accumulating just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed sides. They exit at the round of 32, undone not by any single defeat but by a persistent inability to turn resistance into results.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 thrashing that was effectively settled inside the first minute and ruthlessly extended across both halves. The Africans scored through five different players, the goals arriving in waves rather than any sustained Iraqi resistance. It was the punctuation mark on a tournament Iraq would sooner forget — three defeats, twelve goals conceded, none scored, and elimination from a competition they technically advanced into as a best third-placed side, only to find the step up in class absolute and unforgiving.

Ivory Coast.text: Nicolas Pépé settled the Ivory Coast's group finale early and late — a seventh-minute opener and a composed second on the hour gave the Elephants a clean 2-0 win over Curaçao that owed nothing to fortune. They face Norway in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 10:00 PT, a tie that carries genuine menace: the Scandinavians arrive with a forward line capable of punishing anyone, and Ivory Coast, for all their class, must prove their defensive discipline holds against sharper opposition. Survive that, and Brazil await in the Round of 16 — a rematch of continental pride against the tournament's perennial heavyweights, and the real measure of how far this generation can go.

Japan.text: Japan exit the group stage with genuine credit but also a cautionary tale: they led Brazil through Sano's 29th-minute strike before Casemiro levelled and Martinelli broke their hearts with a 95th-minute winner, a gut-punch finish that flatters neither side's performance. Next up is a Round of 16 meeting with either Ivory Coast or Norway on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT — a tie that looks navigable on paper, though Norway's direct, physical game could expose the same defensive vulnerabilities Brazil found. Should Japan advance, England or Mexico may await in the quarterfinal, where the tournament would begin to ask harder questions of Hajime Moriyasu's side.

Jordan.text:
Jordan's final group-stage outing against Argentina followed a familiar script: two goals down by the half-hour mark, Al-Tamari pulling one back just after the interval to briefly threaten a comeback, before Messi settled the contest with ten minutes to go. It was a respectable showing in impossible company, but the gulf in class was never seriously in doubt.

Winless across three matches and with a goal difference of minus five, Jordan nonetheless advanced as one of the best third-placed sides — only to exit at that stage, their tournament concluding without a knockout fixture to call their own.

Mexico.text: Mexico closed out the group stage in commanding fashion, shutting out the Czech Republic 3-0 with goals either side of the hour mark and a late third to put the scoreline beyond doubt — a thoroughly professional performance from a side that finishes top of Group A with a perfect record and a clean sheet in every match. Ecuador await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a tie Mexico enter as clear favourites against a side that rarely troubles the continental elite, though South American opponents have a habit of making these encounters scrappier than the standings suggest. Should they advance, England or DR Congo loom in the Round of 16, and a quarterfinal against Brazil would be the tie the tournament craves.

Morocco.text: Morocco saw off Haiti in a game that looked comfortable on paper yet required some composure to settle — an own goal conceded inside ten minutes and a second Haitian strike just before half-time kept the scoreline honest until Achraf Hakimi and Saibari struck either side of the break to reassert control, with two late goals adding gloss. The Atlas Lions meet the Netherlands in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 18:00 PT, a genuine examination: Dutch pedigree and depth make them clear favourites, yet Morocco's Qatar 2022 heroics lend them credible underdog menace. Survive that and Canada await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with France — a fixture that would rekindle memories this squad has not forgotten.

Netherlands.text: Netherlands closed out Group F in purposeful fashion, leading Tunisia inside seven minutes through an own goal and Brobbey before a brief Tunisian revival was snuffed out by Hecke's second-half strike — a 3-1 win that flatters neither side nor undersells them. Morocco arrive for the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 18:00 PT as a genuinely awkward opponent, defensively organised and capable of unsettling fancied sides, but the Dutch, as group winners with genuine attacking fluency, carry the weight of expectation. Should they advance through Canada in the Round of 16, a quarterfinal could bring France or Sweden into view — the kind of fixture that would define the tournament's true shape.

New Zealand.text: Belgium dismantled New Zealand with something close to contempt, Trossard scoring either side of half-time before De Bruyne and Lukaku twisted the knife — the All Whites' consolation on 84 minutes a cosmetic gesture against a 5-1 final score. New Zealand's tournament is over: they finish fourth in Group G, their single point earned somewhere earlier in the group stage, the gap in class against Europe's elite ultimately impossible to bridge.

Norway.text: France administered a sobering lesson in the final group game, Ousmane Dembélé completing a first-half hat-trick before Norway had time to find their footing — Aasgaard's brief reply on 21 minutes barely a footnote in a 4-1 drubbing that exposed real defensive frailty. Norway advance to face Ivory Coast in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 10:00 PT, a fixture they will be expected to win, though the Elephants are no pushover and a team this porous in transition cannot afford complacency. Survive that, and Brazil await in the Round of 16 — a brutally early encounter with one of the tournament's heavyweights. Should Norway somehow navigate that mountain, England or Argentina lurk as possibilities further down the road.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was confirmed with a 0-2 defeat to England, a result that flattered neither side in the opening hour before Bellingham and Kane struck in quick succession midway through the second half to put the tie beyond doubt. Three losses, no points, and a four-goal deficit across Group L tells the full story of a tournament in which Panama never found their footing at this level. They depart having shown that the step up to a 48-team World Cup brought them no easier path to the knockout rounds.

Paraguay.text: Paraguay arrived in the knockout rounds the hard way — third in Group D with four points — yet they have just produced the result of the tournament, holding Germany to 1-1 through ninety minutes after Enciso's first-half strike and Havertz's equaliser, then edging the shootout 4-3 to send one of the world's heavyweights home. The Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT brings either France or Sweden, and whichever it is, Paraguay will again be the underdog — though any side that has just beaten Germany on penalties carries a psychological authority that makes this anything but a foregone conclusion. Survive that, and Canada, Morocco, or the Netherlands could await in the quarterfinal — with Spain, Portugal, or Brazil lurking as potential semifinal adversaries for a side that is quietly becoming the draw's most dangerous inconvenience.

Portugal.text: Portugal ground out a goalless draw with Colombia to close the group stage — a flat, purposeful performance that secured qualification without the flourish their earlier form had promised, finishing second in Group K. Croatia await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a fixture that looks favorable on paper given Croatia's diminished standing since their 2018 peak, though Zlatko Dalić's side have a habit of making knockout football deeply uncomfortable. Should Portugal advance, Spain loom as a potential Round of 16 collision — a genuine test of ambition — with Belgium or the United States possible quarterfinal opponents further down a draw that grows considerably more treacherous from the last eight onward.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony, a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina that laid bare the limitations of a side that rarely threatened to trouble the tournament's upper tier. Behind by two goals before half-time following strikes from Alajbegovic and Abunada, Al-Haydos pulled one back on the stroke of the interval to offer a fleeting flicker of hope, only for Mahmic to extinguish it in the 80th minute. The hosts of 2022 finished bottom of Group B and depart with a single point to their name, a tournament record that reflects, honestly enough, where this squad currently stands among the world's best.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde that said little for either side — a flat, scoreless contest that confirmed the Green Falcons' limitations rather than dispelling them. Across three matches they managed a single goal, earned two draws, and finished fourth in Group H, advancing only by the grace of the best third-placed team standings. Their tournament ended there: not in a blaze of controversy or misfortune, but in the quiet arithmetic of a side that rarely threatened and ultimately ran out of road.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament with something close to contempt — Júnior struck twice before half-time, the second a stoppage-time flourish that summarised the gulf in class, and Cunha added a third after the interval to make it a thorough, joyless afternoon. Scotland had squeezed through as one of the better third-placed sides, a minor achievement rendered hollow by the manner of their exit. They leave the 2026 World Cup having won once in three matches, outscored four goals to one, and ultimately exposed by the first genuine elite opposition they faced.

Senegal.text: Senegal closed the group stage with a statement: a 5-0 demolition of Iraq that was effectively settled inside the first four minutes and then ruthlessly extended across the second half, with Gueye helping himself to a brace. That performance was emphatic enough to haul them through as one of the best third-placed sides, though Belgium await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a genuine test, with the Belgians clear favourites and Senegal the side that must prove their Iraq display was character rather than context. Survive that, and a potential Round of 16 collision with the host United States looms, before a quarterfinal draw that could deliver Portugal or Spain as the price of further progress.

South Africa.text: A stoppage-time strike from Eustáquio condemned South Africa to a 1-0 defeat by Canada in the Round of 32 — a cruel ending to a match they may well feel they deserved more from, edged out only by a goal that arrived when the draw seemed the likeliest outcome. Their tournament is over, then, not with a collapse but with a wound: eliminated in the last breath of normal time, denied a place in the last sixteen by the narrowest and most punishing of margins.

South Korea.text: South Korea's group stage ended with a 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's 63rd-minute strike enough to send the Taeguk Warriors out on the wrong note — a flat, uninspiring exit that offered little in mitigation. They had scrapped through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the tournament caught up with them in Atlanta, and a side that never quite found cohesion or cutting edge departed at the first hurdle, their World Cup lasting exactly three matches and yielding just three points.

Spain.text: Spain closed out Group H with the minimum required — a Baena strike just before the break settling a tight, controlled affair against Uruguay, the clean sheet their third in a row and a statement of defensive solidity if not attacking abandon. Austria await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a manageable assignment on paper for a side of Spain's pedigree, though Austrian football has grown genuine teeth and Luis de la Fuente's men will need more invention than they showed here. Should they progress, Croatia or Portugal could loom in the Round of 16 — a potentially mouth-watering Iberian derby or a reunion with a side who know how to hurt them — before a semifinal draw that might yet produce France or the Netherlands.

Sweden.text: Sweden squeezed through Group F in third place on the back of a scrappy, nervy draw with Japan — Maeda putting the Swedes behind before Elanga equalised six minutes later to salvage a point that, just barely, proved enough. Now they face France in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, a fixture that reads as one of the most daunting assignments the last-32 stage can produce; Sweden arrive as clear underdogs against one of the tournament's genuine title contenders, and will need a performance well above anything shown in the group phase. Survive that, and Paraguay await in the Round of 16 — then, potentially, Canada, Morocco, or the Netherlands in the quarterfinal.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland closed out Group B with a purposeful second-half performance against Canada, Vargas and Manzambi scoring within eleven minutes of each other after the break to take firm control before Jonathan David's late consolation added a degree of unnecessary tension. Group winners and in decent nick, they meet Algeria in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT — a winnable assignment for a Swiss side that has long punched at the tournament's harder edges, though Algeria are capable of disrupting rhythm with their own. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana await in the Round of 16; go deeper still, and Argentina could loom in the quarterfinal — a proper examination of just how far this group of Swiss players can travel.

Tunisia.text:
There are a few data issues worth flagging before writing this blurb.

The `status` field says Tunisia "advanced as a best third-placed team out of Group F," but `finish` says "4th place," `eliminated` is true, and the record shows 0 points from three losses — a team that cannot have advanced. The `last_match` also has an apparent error: "Skhiri 3'" and "Hecke 62'" appear in `opponent_goals` (Netherlands), but Skhiri and Hecke are not Dutch players — Skhiri is Tunisian, and Hecke is associated with Belgian/Dutch club football but the own-goal or misattribution here is unclear. Most strikingly, one of Tunisia's listed `opponent_goals` is likely a Tunisian own goal or a data-entry mix-up.

I don't want to invent a clean narrative around contradictory facts. Could you clarify:

1. Did Tunisia finish 4th and exit, or did they genuinely advance?
2. Are the goal attributions correct — specifically, were Skhiri and Hecke own goals credited to Netherlands, or are they misplaced in the wrong scorer list?

Once the brief is consistent, I'll write the blurb accurately.

Turkey.text: Turkey's group stage exit arrived with the cruel drama their tournament deserved — a 3-2 win over the United States that was ultimately rendered meaningless in the standings. The Americans struck first through Trusty inside three minutes, yet Güler and Yilmaz turned things around before half-time, only for Berhalter to level early in the second half; Ayhan's stoppage-time header deep into added time sealed the points but not progress. Turkey finished fourth in Group D, their one victory coming too late and too narrow in goal difference to carry them through, and so a tournament of intermittent promise ends without a knockout stage to show for it.

USA.text: The USA closed their group stage with a bruising 2-3 defeat to Turkey — Trusty's early opener quickly overturned, a second-half Berhalter goal unable to prevent an Ayhan sucker-punch deep in stoppage time that stung but ultimately cost them nothing in the standings. They top Group D regardless, and face Bosnia & Herzegovina in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT — a winnable assignment against a side without the pedigree to trouble a confident American outfit, provided the defensive frailties exposed by Turkey are tightened. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16, with the quarterfinal potentially throwing up a genuine examination from the likes of Portugal or Spain.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Baena's first-half strike proving the solitary difference in a contest that felt tighter than the scoreline suggests — a game Uruguay never quite unlocked despite staying in it until the final whistle. They leave the tournament having advanced as one of the best third-placed teams, only to find no road beyond the group: two draws and a single defeat across three matches tell the story of a side that ground out points without ever imposing themselves, their tournament concluding in the group stage of World Cup 2026.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan signed off from their maiden World Cup with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo that told a familiar story: an early Shomurodov strike offered fleeting promise, before the Congolese took control with a penalty from Wissa, a Mayele effort, and a late third that confirmed the margin was entirely deserved. They finish bottom of Group K without a point, having conceded eleven goals across three matches — a brutal education at football's highest level. Central Asia's standard-bearers depart having shown courage simply in qualifying, but the gap in class exposed here gives the next generation a clear-eyed reckoning to build from.
